name: Aurora vs RDS Architecture
description: Comparing Aurora's cloud-native storage architecture with standard RDS for workload-specific database selection
key_facts: Aurora storage uses a distributed, fault-tolerant cluster volume across 3 AZs with 6 copies of data
Aurora supports up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms replica lag vs RDS read replicas with potentially minutes of lag
Aurora Serverless v2 scales in increments of 0.5 ACU with minimum 0.5 ACU
RDS Multi-AZ uses synchronous block-level replication to a standby for HA, not read scaling
Aurora Global Database provides cross-region replication with typically under 1-second lag
common_misconceptions: RDS Multi-AZ provides read scaling (the standby is not available for reads; use read replicas)
Aurora is always more cost-effective than RDS (for small or simple workloads, standard RDS can be cheaper)

name: DynamoDB Data Modeling
description: Designing efficient partition key, sort key, and secondary index structures for DynamoDB access patterns
key_facts: Partition key determines data distribution; high-cardinality keys prevent hot partitions
Composite primary key (partition + sort key) enables range queries within a partition
Global Secondary Index (GSI) has its own partition and sort key with eventually consistent reads only
Local Secondary Index (LSI) shares the table partition key but uses an alternate sort key; must be created at table creation
Single-table design consolidates multiple entity types using overloaded sort keys to reduce query latency
common_misconceptions: GSIs support strongly consistent reads (they only support eventually consistent reads)
LSIs can be added after table creation (they must be defined at table creation time)

name: Redshift Distribution Styles and Sort Keys
description: Optimizing Redshift table design with distribution styles, sort keys, and compression for analytical workloads
key_facts: KEY distribution co-locates rows with the same distribution key value on the same node for join optimization
EVEN distribution spreads rows uniformly when no clear join key exists
ALL distribution replicates the entire table to every node for small dimension tables
Compound sort keys are best for queries that filter on a prefix of the key columns in order
Interleaved sort keys give equal weight to each column for ad-hoc query patterns
common_misconceptions: AUTO distribution always chooses the optimal strategy (it may not for complex query patterns; manual tuning can outperform it)
Interleaved sort keys are always better than compound (interleaved has higher vacuum overhead and is slower for prefix-based queries)

name: Neptune Graph Database Design
description: Using Amazon Neptune for graph and knowledge graph workloads with Gremlin and SPARQL query languages
key_facts: Supports both Property Graph (Gremlin) and RDF (SPARQL) query models
Optimized for highly connected datasets like social networks, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs
Read replicas provide read scaling with up to 15 replicas across AZs
Neptune Streams captures graph changes for event-driven architectures
Neptune ML integrates with SageMaker for graph neural network predictions
common_misconceptions: Neptune replaces relational databases for all use cases (it is purpose-built for graph traversals and relationship-heavy queries)
Gremlin and SPARQL can query the same data interchangeably (they use different data models: Property Graph vs RDF)

name: DMS and Schema Conversion Tool
description: Using AWS Database Migration Service and Schema Conversion Tool for homogeneous and heterogeneous migrations
key_facts: DMS supports full load, CDC (change data capture), and full load plus CDC migration types
SCT converts schema objects including stored procedures, functions, and views between database engines
DMS replication instance must be sized based on the volume of data and rate of change
Multi-AZ replication instances provide HA for the migration process itself
SCT assessment report identifies conversion complexity and manual intervention needed
common_misconceptions: DMS handles schema conversion automatically (SCT is needed for heterogeneous migrations to convert schema)
CDC captures schema changes (CDC only captures data changes; schema changes require manual handling)

name: RDS Snapshots and Aurora Cloning
description: Database backup, restore, and cloning strategies for deployment and disaster recovery
key_facts: Automated backups enable point-in-time recovery (PITR) with 5-minute granularity
Manual snapshots persist until explicitly deleted; automated backups are deleted with the instance
Aurora cloning uses copy-on-write protocol and creates a clone in minutes regardless of database size
Cross-region snapshot copy enables disaster recovery in another region
Sharing encrypted snapshots across accounts requires sharing the KMS key used for encryption
common_misconceptions: Aurora clones are full copies that consume the same storage (they use copy-on-write and initially share storage)
Automated backups are retained after deleting an RDS instance (they are deleted unless a final snapshot is taken)

name: Cross-Region Replicas and Global Databases
description: Multi-region database architectures for disaster recovery and low-latency global access
key_facts: RDS cross-region read replicas use asynchronous replication with potential minutes of lag
Aurora Global Database provides managed cross-region replication with typically under 1-second lag
DynamoDB Global Tables provide active-active multi-region replication with eventual consistency
Aurora Global Database supports managed planned failover for controlled region switchover
Cross-region replication incurs data transfer costs between regions
common_misconceptions: Aurora Global Database secondaries can serve writes (only the primary region handles writes; secondaries are read-only unless promoted)
DynamoDB Global Tables support strongly consistent reads across regions (cross-region reads are eventually consistent)

name: Parameter Groups and Maintenance Windows
description: Managing database engine configuration and scheduled maintenance for RDS and Aurora
key_facts: DB parameter groups control engine-level settings like max_connections and innodb_buffer_pool_size
DB cluster parameter groups apply to all instances in an Aurora cluster
Some parameter changes require a reboot to take effect (static parameters) while others apply immediately (dynamic parameters)
Maintenance windows define when patches and minor upgrades are applied
Option groups enable additional features like Oracle TDE or SQL Server Audit
common_misconceptions: All parameter changes take effect immediately (static parameters require a reboot; only dynamic parameters apply immediately)
Modifying the default parameter group is best practice (always create a custom parameter group; the default cannot be modified)

name: Performance Insights and RDS Proxy
description: Database performance monitoring and connection management tools for RDS and Aurora
key_facts: Performance Insights visualizes database load by waits, SQL statements, hosts, and users
DB load metric measures Active Sessions normalized by vCPU count
RDS Proxy pools and shares database connections to reduce connection churn
RDS Proxy supports IAM authentication for database access without storing credentials
RDS Proxy improves Lambda function performance by reusing database connections across invocations
common_misconceptions: Performance Insights incurs high cost (free tier retains 7 days of history; longer retention is paid)
RDS Proxy eliminates connection limits (it manages connections more efficiently but the database max_connections limit still applies)

name: CloudWatch RDS Metrics and Enhanced Monitoring
description: Monitoring RDS and Aurora performance using CloudWatch metrics and Enhanced Monitoring for OS-level visibility
key_facts: Standard CloudWatch metrics include CPUUtilization, FreeableMemory, ReadIOPS, WriteIOPS at 1-minute intervals
Enhanced Monitoring provides OS-level metrics at up to 1-second granularity via an agent on the instance
Enhanced Monitoring captures process-level CPU, memory, and I/O not available in standard CloudWatch
CloudWatch Alarms can trigger SNS notifications or Auto Scaling actions based on RDS metrics
DatabaseConnections metric helps detect connection exhaustion before it affects applications
common_misconceptions: Standard CloudWatch provides OS-level metrics for RDS (Enhanced Monitoring is required for OS-level visibility)
Enhanced Monitoring data goes to CloudWatch Metrics (it is published to CloudWatch Logs as JSON documents)

name: Slow Query Logs and Troubleshooting
description: Identifying and resolving database performance issues using query logs and execution plan analysis
key_facts: MySQL slow query log captures queries exceeding the long_query_time threshold
PostgreSQL pg_stat_statements extension tracks execution statistics for all SQL statements
RDS logs can be published to CloudWatch Logs for centralized analysis and alarming
Aurora MySQL audit logs capture database activity for compliance requirements
EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveal query execution plans for optimization
common_misconceptions: Slow query logs are enabled by default (they must be enabled via parameter groups)
Query performance issues are always CPU-related (I/O waits, lock contention, and network latency are equally common causes)

name: Redshift WLM and Query Monitoring
description: Workload management configuration and query monitoring for Amazon Redshift performance optimization
key_facts: WLM queues allocate memory and concurrency slots to different workload types
Automatic WLM dynamically manages memory and concurrency based on workload patterns
Short Query Acceleration (SQA) detects and routes short-running queries to a dedicated pool
Concurrency Scaling adds transient clusters during demand spikes for consistent performance
STL and SVL system tables provide detailed query execution and compilation metrics
common_misconceptions: Manual WLM is recommended over Automatic WLM (Automatic WLM is the AWS-recommended default for most workloads)
Concurrency Scaling is always active (it activates only when queued queries exceed the configured WLM threshold)

name: RDS and DynamoDB Encryption
description: Encryption at rest and in transit for RDS, Aurora, and DynamoDB databases
key_facts: RDS encryption at rest must be enabled at creation time; you cannot encrypt an existing unencrypted instance directly
To encrypt an existing unencrypted RDS instance: create snapshot, copy with encryption, restore from encrypted snapshot
Aurora encrypts all replicas, snapshots, and backups automatically once the cluster is encrypted
DynamoDB encryption at rest is enabled by default using an AWS owned key; customer managed KMS keys are optional
RDS supports SSL/TLS connections; some engines like PostgreSQL can enforce SSL via the rds.force_ssl parameter
common_misconceptions: You can enable encryption on an existing unencrypted RDS instance (you must create an encrypted copy via snapshot)
DynamoDB does not support encryption at rest (it is encrypted by default since 2018)

name: IAM Database Authentication and Audit Logging
description: Token-based database authentication using IAM and database activity auditing
key_facts: IAM DB authentication generates temporary tokens valid for 15 minutes for MySQL and PostgreSQL on RDS and Aurora
SSL/TLS is required when using IAM DB authentication
Database Activity Streams provide near-real-time streams of database activity for compliance
Aurora MySQL advanced audit logs capture DML and DDL operations with configurable detail levels
RDS Proxy supports IAM authentication and forwards connections to the database using stored credentials
common_misconceptions: IAM DB authentication works with all database engines (it only supports MySQL and PostgreSQL on RDS and Aurora)
IAM DB authentication tokens are long-lived (they expire after 15 minutes and must be regenerated)

name: SSL Connections and Network Security
description: Enforcing encrypted connections and network isolation for database instances
key_facts: RDS instances should be deployed in private subnets with no public accessibility
DB subnet groups define which subnets in a VPC can host RDS instances
Security groups control inbound access to RDS instances at the port level
PostgreSQL rds.force_ssl parameter enforces SSL for all connections
MySQL require_secure_transport parameter enforces SSL when set via a custom parameter group
common_misconceptions: RDS instances in a VPC are automatically inaccessible from the internet (Public Accessibility must be set to No explicitly)
SSL is enforced by default on all RDS engines (SSL is available but not enforced by default; it must be configured per engine)
